name: reverse plank with leg lift
instructions: Sit on the ground with your legs extended in front of you and your hands resting on the ground behind you, fingers pointing towards your feet.
Press through your hands and lift your hips off the ground, coming into a reverse plank position.
Engage your core and lift one leg off the ground, extending it straight up towards the ceiling.
Hold for a moment, then lower your leg back down.
Continue alternating legs for the desired number of repetitions.

Sit on the ground with your legs extended in front of you and your hands resting on the ground behind you, fingers pointing towards your feet. Press through your hands and lift your hips off the ground, coming into a reverse plank position. Engage your core and lift one leg off the ground, extending it straight up towards the ceiling. Hold for a moment, then lower your leg back down. Repeat with the other leg. Continue alternating legs for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Keep load symmetrical and stop at positions that create sharp pain or twisting.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Avoid loaded spinal flexion and excessive hinge depth. Brace core and use supported positions.
Prolonged isometric holds can spike blood pressure. Use shorter durations and breathe consistently.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
